mainEntity.name: What is TDS and who must deduct it?

acceptedAnswer.text: Tax Deducted at Source (TDS) is a mechanism where the payer deducts tax at prescribed rates from payments made to the payee and deposits it with the government. Every company, partnership, LLP, and individual/HUF liable for tax audit must deduct TDS on: salary payments (Section 192), contractor payments (Section 194C — 1% individual, 2% others), professional fees (Section 194J — 10%), rent (Section 194I — 10% for land/building), interest on deposits (Section 194A — 10%), and commission/brokerage (Section 194H — 5%). The deductor must obtain a TAN (Tax Deduction Account Number), deduct TDS at the correct rate, deposit by the 7th of the following month (March deductions by April 30), and file quarterly TDS returns (24Q for salary, 26Q for non-salary).

mainEntity.name: What is the penalty for late TDS deposit?

acceptedAnswer.text: TDS penalties are among the most severe in the Income Tax Act: (1) Interest under Section 201(1A): 1% per month from deduction date to deposit date (for late deposit) or 1.5% per month if TDS was not deducted at all. This is simple interest, not compound — but it accumulates rapidly. (2) Late filing fee under Section 234E: ₹200 per day until the return is filed (capped at the TDS amount). (3) Penalty under Section 271C: Amount equal to the TDS that was not deducted. This is in addition to interest. (4) Prosecution under Section 276B: Failure to deposit TDS after deduction — imprisonment of 3 months to 7 years. This is the nuclear provision — it applies to TDS deducted from employees/vendors but NOT deposited with the government. For companies, the director/officer responsible can be prosecuted personally.

mainEntity.name: What is Form 26AS and why is reconciliation critical?

acceptedAnswer.text: Form 26AS is the taxpayer's annual tax statement — it shows all TDS deducted on your behalf by employers, clients, banks, and other deductors; all advance tax and self-assessment tax paid; and all refunds received. The Annual Information Statement (AIS) is the expanded version showing financial transactions (mutual fund purchases, property transactions, foreign remittances, etc.). 26AS reconciliation means verifying that: (a) all TDS deducted by the company (as deductor) appears in the deductees' 26AS — if it doesn't, the company deducted TDS but didn't deposit it (serious compliance failure), and (b) all TDS shown in the company's own 26AS matches TDS certificates received — if less, the company is losing legitimate TDS credit. Virtual Auditor performs quarterly 26AS reconciliation as part of TDS compliance management — catching mismatches before they become demand notices.

mainEntity.name: What are the quarterly TDS return forms and due dates?

acceptedAnswer.text: TDS returns are filed quarterly: Form 24Q — TDS on salary. Due dates: Q1 (Apr-Jun): July 31. Q2 (Jul-Sep): October 31. Q3 (Oct-Dec): January 31. Q4 (Jan-Mar): May 31. Form 26Q — TDS on non-salary payments (contractors, professionals, rent, interest, commission). Same due dates as 24Q. Form 27Q — TDS on payments to non-residents (Section 195). Same due dates. Form 27EQ — TCS (Tax Collected at Source). Same due dates. Each return must reconcile with the challans deposited during the quarter. Mismatches between challan data and return data result in demand notices from CPC-TDS (Ghaziabad). Virtual Auditor handles return preparation, challan-return matching, and Form 16/16A generation.

mainEntity.name: When must Form 16 and Form 16A be issued?

acceptedAnswer.text: Form 16 (salary TDS certificate): Must be issued by the employer to all employees by June 15 following the financial year. Part A: TDS deducted and deposited (generated from TRACES). Part B: Detailed salary breakup, deductions, and tax computation. Form 16A (non-salary TDS certificate): Must be issued to the deductee within 15 days from the due date of TDS return filing. For example: Q1 return due July 31 → Form 16A due by August 15. Non-issuance of Form 16/16A: penalty under Section 272A(2) — ₹100 per day of default per certificate.

mainEntity.name: What is Section 194Q and when does it apply?

acceptedAnswer.text: Section 194Q requires TDS deduction at 0.1% by a buyer on purchase of goods exceeding ₹50 lakh from a seller in a financial year. Applicability: The buyer must have turnover exceeding ₹10 crore in the preceding financial year. Rate: 0.1% on the amount exceeding ₹50 lakh (5% if seller does not furnish PAN). Interaction with TCS: If the seller is liable to collect TCS under Section 206C(1H), and the buyer is liable to deduct TDS under 194Q — TDS under 194Q takes precedence. This section was introduced to widen the tax net and is frequently overlooked by businesses — resulting in demand notices for non-deduction.

Section 197 allows a taxpayer to apply for a certificate for lower or nil TDS deduction if their actual tax liability will be significantly lower than the TDS being deducted. Common scenarios: (1) Export businesses with zero/low tax liability due to 10AA/SEZ deductions — but 194C TDS is deducted on domestic purchases. (2) Companies with brought-forward losses — no tax payable but TDS deducted on all receipts. (3) Startups with 80-IAC deduction. Application: Form 13 filed with the Assessing Officer. If approved, the AO issues a certificate specifying the lower rate (including nil). The certificate is provided to deductors, who then deduct at the lower rate. Virtual Auditor files Form 13 applications and manages the certificate renewal annually.

mainEntity.name: How does TDS compliance connect to GST?

acceptedAnswer.text: TDS and GST interact at multiple points: (1) TDS is deducted on the value EXCLUDING GST (for most sections). For example: invoice of ₹1,18,000 (₹1,00,000 + ₹18,000 GST) → TDS deducted on ₹1,00,000, not on ₹1,18,000. However, if the invoice does not separately show the GST component, TDS is deducted on the full amount. (2) GST TDS under Section 51 of CGST Act: Government entities and PSUs must deduct GST TDS at 2% (1% CGST + 1% SGST) on contracts exceeding ₹2.5 lakh. This is separate from income tax TDS. (3) Reconciliation: Businesses must reconcile income per TDS returns with turnover per GST returns — mismatches trigger notices from both departments.

mainEntity.name: What is the TDS demand from CPC Ghaziabad?

acceptedAnswer.text: CPC-TDS (Centralized Processing Center for TDS) in Ghaziabad processes all TDS returns and issues automated demand notices for: (1) Short deduction — TDS deducted at a lower rate than prescribed. (2) Late deposit — TDS deducted but deposited after the due date (interest under 201(1A)). (3) Non-filing or late filing of returns (fee under 234E). (4) PAN errors — incorrect PAN of deductee resulting in higher TDS rate (20% under Section 206AA). These demands are often automated and may contain errors — Virtual Auditor reviews CPC demands, identifies incorrect charges, files corrections/rectifications, and represents before the TDS Assessing Officer.

mainEntity.name: How much does TDS compliance cost with Virtual Auditor?

acceptedAnswer.text: Monthly TDS compliance (deduction computation, challan preparation, deposit follow-up): ₹5,000-₹15,000/month depending on number of deductees and transaction volume. Quarterly TDS return filing (24Q + 26Q): ₹5,000-₹15,000/quarter. Form 16/16A generation: included in quarterly filing. Annual 26AS reconciliation: ₹10,000-₹25,000. CPC demand response/rectification: ₹10,000-₹30,000 per demand. Form 13 (lower TDS certificate): ₹15,000-₹30,000. Complete TDS package (monthly compliance + quarterly returns + 26AS reconciliation + Form 16): ₹60,000-₹2,00,000/year.

@graph.name: How to Manage TDS Compliance in India

step.name: TAN Registration
step.text: Apply for Tax Deduction Account Number (TAN) through Form 49B on the NSDL website. TAN is mandatory before deducting any TDS.

step.name: Identify TDS Obligations
step.text: Map all payments requiring TDS: salary (192), contractors (194C), professionals (194J), rent (194I), interest (194A), commission (194H), purchase of goods (194Q). Apply correct rates.

step.name: Deduct TDS at Source
step.text: Deduct TDS at the prescribed rate at the time of credit or payment, whichever is earlier. Check for lower deduction certificates (Section 197) from payees.

step.name: Deposit TDS by 7th of Next Month
step.text: Deposit TDS using Challan 281 through online banking or authorized banks. Due date: 7th of the following month (March deductions: April 30). Late deposit: 1.5% interest per month.

step.name: File Quarterly TDS Returns
step.text: File Form 24Q (salary) and 26Q (non-salary) by the quarterly due dates. Reconcile challan data with return data. Correct any PAN errors before filing.

step.name: Issue Form 16/16A
step.text: Generate and issue Form 16 to employees (by June 15) and Form 16A to vendors/professionals (within 15 days of return due date). Download from TRACES portal.

step.name: Quarterly 26AS Reconciliation
step.text: Reconcile TDS deposited (as deductor) with deductees' 26AS. Verify all credits reflect correctly. Identify and resolve mismatches before they become demand notices.
